coredata.dc:title: Co-biopolymer of chitosan/carboxymethyl cellulose hydrogel improved by zinc oxide and graphene quantum dots nanoparticles as pH-sensitive nanocomposite for quercetin delivery to brain cancer treatment
coredata.dc:creator: Ostovar, Shima
Pourmadadi, Mehrab
Zaker, Mohammad Amin
coredata.dc:description: Brain cancer is the major reason of cancer-relevant deaths every year, as it is the most challenging cancer to treat and drug delivery. Quercetin (QUR), as a flavonoid substance found in plants and fruits, has good anticancer and medicinal effects on brain tumors, but its low stability and bioavailability as well as the blood-brain barrier (BBB), prevent it from reaching brain tumors. This research has introduced a nanocomposite made of biocompatible polymers, chitosan, and carboxymethyl cellulose. This co- biopolymer's mechanical and chemical properties and drug-loading capacity have been improved by adding zinc oxide nanoparticles (ZnO NPs). In addition, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were used to improve the chemical properties as well as the ability to penetrate the BBB. The CS/CMC/GQDs/ZnO@QUR nanocomposites have nanoneedle structures with an average size of 219.38 ± 5.21 nm and a zeta potential of −53 mV. The morphology, chemical bonds, and crystallinity of the nanocomposite were examined by FE-SEM, FTIR, and XRD analyses, respectively. By examining the release of QUR, it became apparent that the half-drug release takes about 72 h, which has a much more controlled release than other QUR carriers. Further, the MTT test on U-87 MG and L929 cell lines suggested that this nanocomposite has good anticancer properties and low cytotoxicity compared to the free QUR.

head.ce:title: Co-biopolymer of chitosan/carboxymethyl cellulose hydrogel improved by zinc oxide and graphene quantum dots nanoparticles as pH-sensitive nanocomposite for quercetin delivery to brain cancer treatment

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Engineering, College of Engineering, University of Tehran, Tehran 11155-4563, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemical Engineering, College of Engineering, University of Tehran, Tehran 11155-4563, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Mechanical Engineering, College of Engineering, University of Tehran, Tehran 11155-4563, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Mechanical Engineering, College of Engineering, University of Tehran, Tehran 11155-4563, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Brain cancer is the major reason of cancer-relevant deaths every year, as it is the most challenging cancer to treat and drug delivery. Quercetin (QUR), as a flavonoid substance found in plants and fruits, has good anticancer and medicinal effects on brain tumors, but its low stability and bioavailability as well as the blood-brain barrier (BBB), prevent it from reaching brain tumors. This research has introduced a nanocomposite made of biocompatible polymers, chitosan, and carboxymethyl cellulose. This co- biopolymer's mechanical and chemical properties and drug-loading capacity have been improved by adding zinc oxide nanoparticles (ZnO NPs). In addition, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were used to improve the chemical properties as well as the ability to penetrate the BBB. The CS/CMC/GQDs/ZnO@QUR nanocomposites have nanoneedle structures with an average size of 219.38 ± 5.21 nm and a zeta potential of −53 mV. The morphology, chemical bonds, and crystallinity of the nanocomposite were examined by FE-SEM, FTIR, and XRD analyses, respectively. By examining the release of QUR, it became apparent that the half-drug release takes about 72 h, which has a much more controlled release than other QUR carriers. Further, the MTT test on U-87 MG and L929 cell lines suggested that this nanocomposite has good anticancer properties and low cytotoxicity compared to the free QUR.

ce:section.ce:para: Treatment of brain diseases such as brain cancer, despite their increasing prevalence rate, faces many challenges. Medication protocols are mostly focused on surgery followed by radiation therapy. These protocols are invasive and the average life expectancy is less than two years; so they have poor therapeutical impacts and many side effects [1]. Also, brain drug delivery is generally limited due to the BBB. BBB is a barrier resulting from strong connections between endothelial cells that many drugs and macromolecules are unable to pass through, unlike the normal structure of the capillaries [2,3].

Among different drugs, bioactive compounds have attracted a lot of attention as a natural medicine for cancers [4–6]. One of the flavonoid compounds found in many edible and medicinal plants is quercetin (QUR). QUR has considerable potential as an antioxidant and anti-neuroinflammatory agent for the treatment of brain cancer [7]. Several studies have noted that QUR powerfully reduces tumor cell progression using molecular mechanisms including necrosis, antiproliferative activity, apoptosis, and suppression of tumor invasion and migration [8–11]. Considering the poor bioavailability of QUR along with the limitation of passing through the BBB, its brain drug delivery needs attention.

A drug delivery strategy for cancer cells is pH-sensitive targeted drug delivery using hydrogels. Due to oncogenic metabolism, cancerous cells undergo a more acidic environment than normal cells, so this creates a potential for using polymers that release their cargo more in an acidic environment than in an alkaline environment [12,13]. Chitosan (CS) is a natural pH-sensitive polymer, which releases its cargo because of the protonation of amino functional groups in an acidic environment [14,15]. CS can open the junctions among epithelial cells reversibly and accelerate the drugs and cargo transport [16]. In addition, carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC) is an anionic polymer that has excellent features such as hydrophilicity, bio-adhesion, non-toxicity, and good gelling ability which introduced CMC as a good option for use in drug delivery [17,18]. The combination of CS and CMC creates a stable polyelectrolyte composite because of the interaction between cationic and anionic groups of CS and CMC [19].

In many reports, there is the use of carbon or carbon-based nanomaterials in biotechnological applications [20–22]. Among carbon-based NPs, graphene has confirmed excellent properties for drug delivery applications, such as improving delivery efficiency and increasing therapeutic effects [23–27]. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have more favorable properties, including higher solubility in water, less cytotoxicity, and higher surface area, which makes their use in drug delivery applications more widespread [28–30]. The utilization of GQD in nanocarriers controls the kinetics of release and increases the circulation time of the drug [31,32]. GQD has a unique feature for imaging cancer tumors that can significantly enhance signals. Much research has been devoted to the use of GQDs for PDT, PTT, and guided multimodal imaging in addition to their application as multifunctional carriers for the treatment and imaging of cancerous tumors [30]. In addition to favorable physicochemical properties, employing GQDs in drug carriers' structures for cancer treatment can accelerate and increase the DNA damaging effect [33,34]. Also, GQDs cause fluorescence properties in nanocarriers, which can be useful for cancer theragnostic applications [35]. GQDs alone have a proven effect on the treatment and prevention of nervous system diseases [36,37]. For example, GQDs are covalently bonded to tramiprosate effectively inhibiting the accumulation of amyloid-β, which ultimately leads to the prevention and treatment of Alzheimer's disease [38,39]. GQDs also have a therapeutic effect on Parkinson's disease [40]. Among the characteristics of GQDs that distinguish them is their ability to pass and penetrate the BBB which makes them more useful in brain disease drug delivery [41–44].

Adding metal-derived NPs to hydrogels improves the physicochemical, mechanical, and biological properties of the polymer [45–50]. This improvement is mainly due to the enhancement of the stability by physical cross-linking and the increase of the specific surface of hydrogels, which leads to an improvement in the ability to drug loading and controlled drug release [51–53]. Several metal oxide NPs are being developed for cancer treatment application, but zinc oxide nanoparticles (ZnO NPs) have remarkably demonstrated their potential in the treatment of different types of cancers because of their biocompatibility, biodegradability, and unique physico-chemical attributes [54]. Besides these excellent properties, ZnO NPs show favorable properties for cancer treatment application. Many research showed anticancer effect of ZnO NPs, which has been proven to be mainly due to the creation of reactive oxygen species (ROS) followed by the destruction of the membrane of mitochondria and apoptosis of cancerous tumors [55,56]. Therefore, ZnO NPs are widely used in the structure of cancer drug delivery composites [54,57,58]. Sathishkumar et al. [59] made a ZnO-QUR nanocomposite and proved its anticancer properties. Their findings demonstrated that ZnO NPs significantly inhibit the growth of breast cancer cells compared to pure QUR.

Many nanocarriers have been designed and fabricated for QUR delivery (Table 1 ). Sun et al. [60] successfully delivered QUR to the brain for Alzheimer's treatment using PLGA-based nanocarrier. Their results suggested the treatment of the animal Alzheimer model with PLGA@QUR NPs was effective. Also, Nanaki et al. [61] designed a PLGA-based nanocarrier with an average size of 394 nm and a zeta potential of −29 mV that successfully passes through BBB. In this research, we designed a nanocomposite by adding GQDs and ZnO NPs to CS and CMC hydrogel for QUR delivery. Therefore, CS/CMC/GQDs/ZnO@QUR nanocomposites were made by the double emulsion method (Water/Oil/Water) to increase the QUR bioavailability and its targeted delivery to brain cancerous tumors. The amount of drug loading and drug encapsulation in this nanocomposite and the effect of ZnO on the amount of drug loading were measured. Next, by FE-SEM, XRD, and FTIR analysis, the crystalline and amorphous structure, size, and morphology of the nanocomposites were investigated. After that, by observing the QUR release in two acidic environments (an environment of a cancerous cell) and an alkaline environment (an environment of healthy tissue), the pattern of drug release was investigated, and the pH sensitivity of the CS/CMC/GQDs/ZnO@QUR nanocomposites was discussed. By observing the drug release pattern, research on the kinetic mechanism of drug release was done by using curve fitting assessment. In the end, the anticancer properties and biocompatibility of CS/CMC/GQDs/ZnO@QUR nanocomposites were investigated by MTT test on U-87 MG and L929 cell lines.

CS (MW = 50 kDa), CMC (MW = 90 kDa), MTT powder, and PBS (pH 7.4) were obtained from Merck company. Also, QUR, citric acid, Span-80, acetic acid, dimethyl sulfoxide, ethanol, and ethyl acetate were purchased from Sigma Co. U-87 MG (human malignant glioma cell) and L929 (mouse fibroblast cell line) cell lines were obtained from Pasture Institute, Iran.
10 g of citric acid is melted by heating at 200 °C for 5 min, and then the derived yellow liquid changes color to orange after 30 min, which indicates the formation of GQDs [62].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CS/CMC/GQDs/ZnO nanocomposites and QUR-loaded nanocomposites
ce:section.ce:para: In an initial stage, 0.3 g of CS was dissolved in 15 mL aqueous acetic acid-contained solution (2 % v/v), so that it was completely dissolved on a stirrer at 20 °C and a gel-like liquid of 2 % CS was collected [28]. In the next step, 0.15 g of CMC was added. The mixture was put in an ultrasonic bath for 30 min [24]. Therefore, 15 mg of GQDs NPs and 15 mg of ZnO NPs were added, and after that, it was thoroughly homogenized on the stirrer.

In the next stage, we loaded the amount of QUR in such a manner that its ending concentration was 5 μg.mL−1 and set it on a stirrer for 30 min to be blended. In the end, a CS/CMC/GQDs/ZnO hydrogel containing QUR was obtained [63]. In the next step, using a sampler, 2 % v/v span 80 as surfactant was added and hazelnut oil as hydrophobic phase was added drop by drop to the homogenized CS/CMC/GQDs/ZnO@QUR while forcefully stirring on the stirrer. 15 min later, 15 mL of distilled water drop by drop was added for washing, after 10 min, the solution was removed from the stirrer and allowed the layers to form for 15 min. Then, a centrifuge was used at 5000 rpm for 5 min to divide CS/CMC/GQDs/ZnO@QUR nanocomposites from the aqueous phase [41,64]. To dry samples, the nanocomposites froze at −20 °C in the refrigerator, after that the samples were lyophilized using a freeze dryer. The graphical overview of the fabrication process of CS/CMC/GQDs/ZnO@QUR nanocomposite is shown in Fig. 1 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of QUR encapsulation efficiency (EE%) and loading efficiency (LE%)
ce:section.ce:para:
To calculate the amount of QUR in nanocomposite (LE% and EE%), we dispersed 1 mg of lyophilized nanocomposite into 1 mL of PBS and then put it on the stirrer and added 1 mL of ethyl acetate. By separating the ethyl acetate phase, the drug can be released [63]. In addition, to compare the impact of adding ZnO NPs in increasing LE% and EE% of QUR, the amount of QUR loaded in CS/CMC/GQDs nanocomposites also was measured. The concentration of QUR was determined by employing a UV–visible spectrophotometer at a wavelength of 370 nm. The following equation describes the approach to calculating LE% and EE%:

ce:section.ce:section-title: Calculation of in vitro QUR release from NPs
ce:section.ce:para:
The release of QUR from nanocomposites was investigated with the help of a dialysis bag at the pH of healthy body tissue (pH 7.4) and the pH of cancerous tissue (pH 5.4) at body temperature (37 °C). The acidic environment was adjusted to pH 5.4 by adding acetic acid to distilled water, and PBS was used for the alkaline solution and simulating the physiological environment of the body. Also, to increase the solubility of QUR, 20 % v/v ethanol is added to the solutions. We pour these two solutions into a falcon and place it in a beaker at 37 °C. After that, we filled the nanocomposites inside the dialysis bag and placed it inside the falcon. At times 1, 2, 3, 6, 12, 24, 48, 72 and 96 h, we took 1 mL of the mixture inside the falcon and 1 mL of fresh solution was replaced [41,65]. The extent of QUR released from the samples was determined with a spectrophotometer at a wavelength of 370 nm [63]. Also, the cumulative release of QUR has been obtained using the standard curve of QUR.

A release profile from CS/CMC/GQDs/ZnO@QUR nanocomposite was obtained in acidic and alkaline conditions. For the statistical analysis of the release mechanism of QUR, using the well-known curve fitting method in drug delivery science in kinetic models, the best fitting kinetic models have been calculated with maximum R2 [66,67].

Cytotoxicity in vitro for QUR, CS, CMC/CS, CS/CMC/GQDs, CS/CMC/GQDs/ZnO and CS/CMC/GQDs/ZnO@QUR nanocomposites on U-87 MG and L929 cell lines employing MTT method was investigated. U-87 MG (human malignant glioma cell) and L929 (mouse fibroblast cell line) cell lines were obtained from Pasture Institute, Iran and cultured in DMEM medium [68].

The culture medium contains 1 % PEN and STR as antibiotics, 10 % FBS and 5 % CO2, adjusted at 37 °C. To form a suitable density (5 × 103 cells per well) in a 96-well plate, the cells were cultured for 24 h in this culture medium. Then, the control cells were incubated without anything and the other cells were incubated with the samples for 72 h. In the next step, the cells were incubated for 4 h by adding 100 μl of MTT reagent. Then, after washing with PBS, dimethyl sulfoxide was added and the well plates were shaken, which improves the solubility of formazan crystals [69]. At the end, the viability of cells was measured utilizing an ELISA microplate reader at a 570 nm wavelength, and the viability percentage was calculated with the below equation:
Percentage of Cell viability =
Absorbance
samples
Absorbance

× 100

Zeta potential analysis was used to measure surface charges. Surface charges indicate repulsion or electrostatic attraction between nanocomposites and therefore their stability. The value of zeta potential is shown in Fig. 3, where an average value of −53 mV was measured as the surface charge for CS/CMC/GQDs/ZnO@QUR nanocomposites. This value is higher than the critical value, so these nanocomposites are dispersed stably.

FTIR spectra for raw materials were presented in Fig. 4 . In the pure CS's spectrum, the peaks about 3351 and 3284 cm−1 were attributed to OH and NH. The narrow peak at 2852 cm−1 was related to CH groups. A peak at 1585 cm−1 referred to the carbonyl functional group. A broad peak is observed between 1277 and 1440 cm−1 and between 900 and 1100 cm−1 associated with CN and CO bonding on the polysaccharide backbone, respectively. In the raw CMC, the peaks at 1632 and 1408 cm−1 observed that are contributed to –COO symmetrical and asymmetrical groups, respectively. The peaks at 3450 cm−1 are associated with hydroxyl group vibration. Also, The CH2 stretching vibration is placed at 2924 cm−1. In the FTIR spectrum of GQDs, peaks approximately about 2854–2924 cm−1 were associated with CH bonding in aromatic rings. Also, the aromatic rings skeletal vibration is located around 1404 and 1726 cm−1. In zinc oxide's spectra, a strong peak was detected at 400–600 cm−1 that is associated with metal oxide bending vibrations.

Compared with spectra of raw materials, the original hydroxyl signal peaks of CMC and CS change into broader peaks and transferred to a higher frequency from 3450 and 3351 cm−1 to 3498 cm−1 that is because of the creation of hydrogen bonding across the –COOH groups of CMC and –OH groups of CS or superposition of stretching vibrations of amine and hydroxyl functional groups. Some small peaks at about 1300–1800 cm−1 were sharpened and an extra bond at 1554 cm−1 was discovered, which could be related to interactions between the cationic –NH groups in CS and the anionic –COOH groups in CMC. In the spectrum belonging to CS/CMC/GQDs composite, the peaks around 1485–1800 cm−1 have been broader which is related to hydrogen bonding between GQDs and CS/CMC hydrogel.

The addition of ZnO NPs to the CS/CMC/GQDs composite caused the peak intensity around 600 cm−1 to remarkably reduced as against the spectrum of ZnO NPs, that approved the composite. Furthermore, the peaks related to OH bonding shifted and sharpened indicating hydrogen bonding between ZnO NPs and CS/CMC/GQDs composite. The addition of QUR to the composite caused the creation of a peak of about 1275 cm−1 stating a bond between Si and the -OH of QUR. Additionally, the peak shifts related to the hydroxyl group at 3500 cm−1 demonstrated the interactions among the carboxyl and carbonyl groups of QUR and composite. The peak at the fingerprint region was related to the characteristic peaks of QUR.

XRD patterns for pure materials and composites are shown in Fig. 5 . GQDs and CMC's XRD pattern shows their amorphous structure (Fig. 5.a and c). Also, CS has a peak at 20°, which is due to its natural polymer network (Fig. 5.b). An effective parameter in the CS's degree of crystallinity is its deacetylation degree [70], and therefore with the intensity of this peak, its deacetylation degree can be calculated so that 82 % deacetylation was calculated for this case [71]. The XRD pattern of CMC has two peaks at 2θ = 20.8° and 2θ = 37.5°, which indicates its amorphous structure. After the combination of CMC and CS, a broad peak is observed at 2θ = 22.1°. This peak has lower intensity compared to pure CMC and CS; it can be concluded that these polymers were well mixed.

The crystal structure for ZnO can be seen according to its peaks in 2θ = 30.21°, 34.15°, 36.87°, 47.13°, 56.78°, 62.78°, 73.18°, and 76.94°, which is related to 100, 002, 101, 102, 110, 103, 112, 202 hexagonal wurtzite nanostructure's planes, respectively [72]. Also, using the Debye–Scherrer equation, the mean size of ZnO crystals was calculated at approximately 41 nm.

In the XRD pattern corresponding to GQDs, there is a broad peak of about 2θ = 20.3°, which indicates the excellent carbonization of citric acid and the formation of a hexagonal graphite plane (002). This reaction is confirmed by the absence of peaks related to citric acid [73]. After adding GQD to CS/CMC hydrogel, its amorphous structure is reduced, and crystalline nanocomposites are obtained. The spectra of CMC/CS/GQDs nanocomposite show a broad peak at 2θ = 16°-25.2° that is because of hydrogel's nature amorphous structure [74]. After adding ZnO NPs, the crystal structure and the location of the peaks remain almost constant, but the intensity of the peaks increases and reaches from 9000 to 14,400 indicating miscibility of CS/CMC/GQD/ZnO nanocomposites [41]. The addition of QUR to CS/CMC/GQDs/ZnO nanocomposite structure causes the broadening of XRD peaks around 2θ = 20° and thus the structure becomes more amorphous. This is primarily due to the presence of QUR on the surface of the nanocomposite.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Calculation of QUR EE% and LE%
ce:section.ce:para: QUR has a considerably low solubility in water, which greatly reduces its bioavailability. A solution for this problem is the utilization of nanocarriers to target drug delivery to cancerous tumors and increase its absorption, which can significantly increase its effect on cancer treatment [75]. For CS/CMC/GQDs nanocomposite as QUR nanocarrier, drug loading and encapsulation efficiency were measured as 38.5 % and 76 %, respectively. Adding ZnO NPs to this nanocomposite increases the amount of EE to 87.5 % and LE to 46.75 % (Table 1). As investigated by Choi et al. [76], a hydrogen bond is created between ZnO NPs and the hydroxyl and amine group of the polymers, which makes the nanocomposite's structure stronger and, as a result, the drug more encapsulated.

The dialysis approach has been utilized to examine the process of QUR release from nanocomposite at body temperature (37 °C) and double pH medium (normal body's pH and the tumor environment's pH). As it is clear in Fig. 6 , due to the pH-sensitive manner of the nanocomposite, at a normal body's pH (7.4), drug release is much slower, so that after 72 h, around half of the drug was released (49 %), while in during this period of the time, 82 % of QUR was released in the tumor environment (more acidic environment). Also, at the end of 96 h, 95 % of the QUR was released in an acidic environment, but just 56 % of QUR was released in an alkaline environment, which proves the sensitivity of the nanocomposite to the pH difference.

In comparing and examining the release half-life (the time that the composite releases half of its cargo), CS/CMC/GQDs/ZnO@QUR nanocomposites have a half-life of <24 h in an acidic environment and >72 h in an alkaline environment. In the study of Hazra et al. [77], which used the combination of Alg and Cs for QUR pH-sensitive drug delivery, in the optimal state with EE of 81 %, at pH 7.4, the nanocomposites have a half-life of 5 h and release 80 % of its drug in 7 h. Also, with the comparison made in Table 1, it can be concluded that CS/CMC/GQDs/ZnO nanocomposite, as a carrier for QUR drug, makes its release much more controlled and due to its pH-sensitive property, it can be targeted on cancerous cells and do not affect healthy cells.

Kinetic evaluations have been done using statistical analysis on the release data at two pH. Drug release data from CS/CMC/GQDs/ZnO@QUR nanocomposites was fitted to zero and first-order, Hixon-Croll, Korsmeyer-Peppas, and Higuchi kinetic models for defining the dominant mechanism of release. By comparing the R2 value, as shown in Table 2 , the best fitting in both acidic and alkaline environments is with the Korsmeyer-Peppas model (whereas for pH 5.4, R2 = 0.977 and for pH 7.4, R2 = 0.988). By plotting the data of QUR release from the nanocomposites as an
C t
C ∞
× 100
versus ln(t), lines with n slopes were obtained (Table 3 ). As stated by Singhvi et al. [78], the slopes of the lines <0.45 (whereas for acidic pH, n = 0.28 and for pH 7.4, n = 0.365) indicate that the release follows the Fick diffusion mechanism. In Fickian diffusion, the drug is mainly diffused due to a concentration gradient at the molecular scale.

The biocompatibility of the nanocomposites was investigated on the L929 cell lines. As seen in Fig. 7 , CS and CS/CMC hydrogels are biocompatible due to 100 % cell viability. The addition of GQDs caused a small decrease in cell viability, which confirms the biocompatibility of CS/CMC/GQDs composite with 99 % cell viability. Also, in the CS/CMC/GQDs/ZnO composite, the effect of adding ZnO on the biocompatibility of the composite has been investigated, which has reduced the viability of L929 cells by only 3 % compared to the ZnO-free composite. Therefore, CS/CMC/GQDs/ZnO composites showed 94 % L929 cell viability; which makes them a biocompatible carrier for drug delivery. Also, CS/CMC/GQDs/ZnO@QUR nanocomposites have a low cytotoxicity effect on L929 cell lines, and cell viability was measured at 85 %.

Cytotoxicity on cancerous cells shows the nanocomposite's ability to inhibit the proliferation of brain tumors and cause their apoptosis, which was investigated on U-87 MG cell lines. The addition of GQDs caused a slight decrease in cell viability (98 %). Also, the addition of ZnO decreases the viability to 94 %. By loading the drug into the nanocomposites, it can be seen that the proliferation of U-87 MG cancer cells achieved 49 %, which showed 13 % more inhibitory effects than free QUR, which indicates much better and more targeted anti-cancer properties of drug-containing nanocomposites comparing free QUR. These results showed that these nanocarriers have significant effects on the treatment of brain cancer and low toxicity on healthy cells.

In this research, a nanocomposite with favorable characteristics was made by blending CS and CMC as pH-sensitive polymers that combined with GQD and ZnO NPs. It was found great nanocomposite for delivering the QUR as an anticancer drug to brain tumors in a targeted and controlled manner. CS/CMC/GQD/ZnO nanocarrier was made by the W/O/W method with 219.38 nm average particle size. The zeta potential was measured at −53 mV. Also, the nanocomposite structure was observed using FE-SEM analysis in the form of nanoneedles, which was confirmed by XRD analysis. In XRD analysis, the amorphous structure is improved by the addition of QUR.

The drug that could be loaded in CS/CMC/GQD/ZnO nanocomposite was measured >46 %. Also, the encapsulation of the drug (EE%) by adding ZnO NPs increased significantly from 76 % to 87.5 %. QUR is released from the nanocomposite in an acidic environment with a half-life of 24 h and in an alkaline environment with a half-life of 72 h, which is much more controlled. In comparison with nanocarriers made for QUR, it was observed that this nanocomposite has a much longer half-life in both acidic and alkaline environments. The release kinetic analysis was consistent with the Korsmeyer-Peppas model with a line slope of <0.45, which indicates the QUR release by the Fick diffusion mechanism. It is mainly because of the significant number of hydrogen bonds created between the two polymers CS and CMC and between the NPs and the polymer structure, which was shown by FTIR evaluations. These hydrogen bonds create a strong structure in the nanocomposite, which improves drug loading and more controlled release.

MTT analysis was carried out on two cell lines to investigate the effect of toxicity and anticancer properties. CS/CMC/GQD/ZnO@QUR nanocomposites had 13 % more tumor proliferation inhibitory effect on brain cancer cells than the pure QUR. Also, CS/CMC/GQD/ZnO@QUR nanocomposites showed acceptable toxicity (85 %) on the L929 cell line, which was 2 % more than the pure QUR.

Therefore, the CS/CMC/GQD/ZnO@QUR nanocomposite is considered an excellent platform for encapsulating QUR, which results in controlled release, increased bioavailability, and drug circulation time in the body. This nanocomposite showed a great anticancer effect on brain cancer cells beyond low toxicity on normal cells, which makes it a safe and powerful therapeutic agent carrier for a challenging cancer such as brain cancer. This nanocomposite indicates a promising future for the combination of co-biopolymers with metal NPs and nano quantum dots for brain drug delivery applications.

Shima Ostovar: Investigation, Resources, Writing – original draft. Mehrab Pourmadadi: Supervision, Validation, Investigation, Methodology, Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing. Mohammad Amin Zaker: Methodology, Data curation, Resources.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
